title: Te Whakamarumaru o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe that features in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about the taniwha Hinekōtukutuku. This awa provided traditional mahinga kai and continues to hold cultural significance for our community.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific testing methods
Compare contemporary scientific data with traditional mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health
Develop a kaitiakitanga action plan proposing evidence-based solutions to address identified environmental issues
Present findings to whānau and community stakeholders using both scientific and cultural frameworks

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Begin at the stream with a karakia and learn the pūrākau of Hinekōtukutuku from a local kaumatua. Students make initial observations and sketch the stream environment, noting cultural landmarks and potential areas of concern.

activities.title: He Tātari Wai - Water Testing Mahi
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and dissolved oxygen at multiple sites along the awa. They record data systematically and learn proper scientific sampling techniques.

activities.title: Mātauranga Māori me te Pūtaiao
activities.description: Compare scientific data with traditional indicators of stream health - presence of native fish (īnanga), watercress (kōwhitiwhiti), clarity for seeing the stream bed. Students create Venn diagrams showing where mātauranga and science align and differ.

activities.title: He Rautaki Kaitiaki - Kaitiakitanga Strategy
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze their findings to identify the biggest threats to the awa's health. They research and propose specific, evidence-based actions (e.g., riparian planting, community education, pollution prevention) to address these issues.

activities.title: Whakanui i te Awa - Community Presentation
activities.description: Groups create digital or physical presentations that share their scientific findings, cultural understandings, and kaitiakitanga proposals with whānau and community representatives at the marae.

resources: Kaumatua Hōri Waititi (local Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge holder)
NIWA River Water Quality Monitoring Kit (pH, nitrate, turbidity tests)
'Te Mātauranga o te Wai' by Dr. Dan Hikuroa (Māori science perspectives)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream

tasks: Water quality data collection and analysis workbook
Comparative report on scientific and mātauranga Māori indicators
Kaitiakitanga action plan proposal with evidence-based recommendations

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in Science - Students will carry out science investigations using a variety of approaches
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth Systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interactions with other earth systems
Science in the New Zealand Curriculum: Participating and Contributing - Students will use their science knowledge when considering issues of concern to Māori
